core_intuitions: The residual stream is an additive shared bus: every attention/MLP sub-layer reads from it and ADDS back, so the value at any layer is embedding + sum of all prior contributions, and any downstream quantity (a logit, an attention score) decomposes linearly into per-component contributions. Attention moves information ACROSS positions; the MLP processes WITHIN a position.

Superposition: a model wants far more features than it has dimensions, so it packs n >> d_model sparse features as non-orthogonal (almost-orthogonal) directions, exploiting the fact that only a few fire per token. The unavoidable price is polysemantic neurons - a single neuron responds to a mix of unrelated features - which is why you cannot read a network neuron-by-neuron.

Probing and the logit lens are OBSERVATIONAL: probing shows whether a feature is linearly decodable at a layer; the logit lens unembeds an intermediate residual to show the prediction sharpening across depth. Both measure correlation/presence, not use - you must intervene to claim causation.

Activation patching turns correlation into cause: run the corrupted input but inject one clean activation (denoising = is it sufficient?) or the reverse (noising = is it necessary?), and measure recovery to localize behavior to a (layer, position, component). Factual recall shows a two-site early(subject-MLP)/late(final-token-attention) pattern.

Circuits are reverse-engineered algorithms over heads/MLPs that compose THROUGH the residual stream (induction heads, the IOI name-movers), and they contain backup/redundant components that make naive ablation under-estimate a head's role - which is why denoising and noising can disagree.

Sparse autoencoders decompose superposition: a wide, sparsely-active hidden layer reconstructs a dense polysemantic activation as a sum of a few monosemantic feature directions, giving a vocabulary for monitoring and steering - but features are lossy hypotheses to be causally validated, not oracles.

existing.name: mechinterp-residual-stream-bus
existing.issue: The figure correctly shows the additive stack and the cross-position vs within-position distinction, but (1) in dark mode the right-hand annotation 'the residual stream is a d_model-wide bus...' overlaps and collides with the dashed bracket, cramping and partly obscuring the text; (2) the two horizontal arrows ('reads across positions' and 'attention mixes across the sequence') are redundant and slightly confusing; (3) the deepest idea of the section - the residual stream as a shared COMMUNICATION channel where an early component writes a subspace that a much later component reads (the 'virtual weight' connection), with intermediate layers leaving it untouched - is only stated in the side text, never shown.
existing.improvement: Fix the dark-mode overlap by giving the right-hand annotation its own clear padded column (or drop the dashed bracket). Collapse the two cross-position arrows into a single unambiguous statement 'attention mixes across positions; the MLP stays within a position'. Make the bus/virtual-weight metaphor concrete: highlight a thin colored sub-band running vertically through the residual stack that an early component (e.g. Attn_0) WRITES and a later component (e.g. MLP_1 or the unembed) READS, with the intermediate blocks visibly leaving that band untouched - so the reader sees communication through a shared subspace, not just an additive tower.

existing.name: mechinterp-patching-recovery-heatmap
existing.issue: Reads clearly in both themes and nails the two-bump early-site/late-site factual-recall pattern with the dashed 'fact moved by attention' arrow and worked recovery number. Only minor nit: in dark mode the two header lines ('clean logit-diff...', 'corrupt logit-diff...') carry heavy light highlight boxes that feel a touch harsh, but they remain legible.
existing.improvement: Optional polish only: soften the dark-mode header highlight boxes so they don't visually shout; no structural change needed.

existing.name: mechinterp-ioi-circuit
existing.issue: Conveys the three functional head classes writing/reading the residual stream, the copy of ' Mary' to the unembed, and the crucial backup-name-mover redundancy that explains why noising and denoising disagree. Minor: in dark mode the grey backup-head box slightly mutes its italic caption, and the '3. copy Mary' label crowds the 'activates on ablation' note.
existing.improvement: Optional polish only: give the backup-head box a hair more vertical clearance from the '3. copy Mary' label and lighten its dark-mode fill so the italic caption reads cleanly; no structural change needed.

new_figures.name: mechinterp-superposition-geometry
new_figures.concept: Why a model packs n >> d_model sparse features into d_model dimensions as non-orthogonal directions, and why that geometry forces individual neurons to be polysemantic.
new_figures.rationale: Superposition is the single most load-bearing idea in the chapter - it is the reason neurons are uninterpretable, the reason SAEs exist (S6), and it is invoked in the Key Takeaways and the limits section - yet it currently has NO figure. The intuition is fundamentally geometric (almost-orthogonal packing + sparsity + a neuron axis cutting across several feature directions) and a static diagram makes it click in a way the prose cannot. This is the highest-value missing visual in the chapter.
new_figures.placement: Section '### 1.3 Superposition: why neurons are polysemantic', immediately after the paragraph that ends '...the expected squared interference scales with k, not n.'
new_figures.brief: A three-panel static infographic, left to right, sharing one low-dimensional space so the reader compares apples to apples. Use a small illustrative dimension (d = 3) and small illustrative feature counts; invent NO benchmark numbers. PANEL A - 'If features were orthogonal (the luxury we don't have)': a small clean 3-axis coordinate frame, each of the 3 perpendicular axes labeled as one feature (e.g. 'quote', 'US-state', 'Python'). Caption: 'd dimensions hold exactly d features - read each off with a clean projection.' PANEL B - 'Superposition (what really happens)': the SAME 3-D space drawn as a disk/sphere now crowded with many more unit arrows (about 8-10) radiating at small angles to one another - visibly 'almost orthogonal', not perpendicular. Annotate the small angle between two arrows with the interference bound (inner product <= epsilon). Add a note 'n >> d features packed in; capacity grows ~ exp(epsilon^2 * d)'. Show SPARSITY explicitly: render only k of the arrows as 'active/lit' on this token (e.g. 2 bright, the rest dimmed) with caption 'only a few fire at once, so interference stays small (scales with k, not n)'. PANEL C - 'The consequence: a polysemantic neuron': isolate ONE coordinate axis and label it 'neuron i (one MLP coordinate)'. Draw dashed projection lines showing this single axis has non-zero overlap with THREE unrelated feature arrows - label them with the chapter's own example, 'academic citations', 'HTTP headers', 'Korean text'. Beside it a tiny 'fires for:' readout listing all three. Caption: 'the neuron is not the unit of computation - the feature DIRECTION, spread across many neurons, is.' Flow arrows between panels: A->B labeled 'more features than dimensions forces packing', B->C labeled 'non-orthogonal packing tangles every neuron'. Theme-safe: use at most two accent hues (one for 'active' feature, one neutral for 'inactive'); no hardcoded primary colors; ensure all arrows, angle labels, and the projection dashes stay inside the viewBox with no overlapping text; clear left-to-right reading order.

new_figures.name: mechinterp-sae-decompose-superposition
new_figures.concept: A sparse autoencoder maps one dense, polysemantic activation into a wide, mostly-zero code whose few active entries are crisp monosemantic feature directions; the reconstruction is a sparse sum of decoder columns.
new_figures.rationale: SAEs are the payoff of the superposition story and the tool behind steering and safety monitoring (S6, S7), but the section is dense equations and code with no visual. The core intuition - 'take a dense tangled vector and re-express it as a short sum of a few clean feature directions from a much wider dictionary' - is exactly the kind of thing a single well-designed static frame conveys instantly and equations do not. It also visually closes the loop opened by the superposition figure (dense tangle in, sparse monosemantic features out).
new_figures.placement: Section '### 6.1 The architecture and objective', immediately after the loss equation block (reconstruction + lambda * L1) and before the sentence beginning 'The l1 penalty forces the code...'
new_figures.brief: A left-to-right static pipeline infographic with a small vector-addition inset; small illustrative sizes only, no invented benchmark numbers. STAGE 1 - INPUT x in R^d_model: a short column of about 6 cells, ALL non-zero with varied small shading, labeled 'residual activation x - dense, polysemantic (a blend of many features)'. STAGE 2 - ENCODER: an arrow labeled 'W_enc, then ReLU / TopK' feeding a MUCH WIDER column, the sparse code f in R^d_sae, about 24 cells tall, of which only 2-3 are lit and the rest are zero/greyed. Caption on this column: 'expansion 8x-32x wider, but only k fire per token (sparse)'. Label each of the 2-3 ACTIVE features with a crisp human concept drawn from the chapter: 'Golden Gate Bridge', 'code that is buggy', 'DNA sequence'. Add a small tag 'ReLU/TopK enforces sparsity - only the few genuinely present features survive'. STAGE 3 - DECODER and RECONSTRUCTION: an arrow labeled 'W_dec (columns = feature DIRECTIONS)' to an output column x_hat the same height as x, labeled 'reconstruction x_hat'. Beside/below it place a small inset showing vector addition: 2-3 labeled arrows (one per active feature, scaled by its activation) summing tip-to-tail to approximately reproduce x - caption 'x_hat = sum over the few active features of (activation x decoder direction)'. BOTTOM RIBBON: the objective 'L = ||x - x_hat||^2 (reconstruction) + lambda * ||f||_1 (sparsity)', with a small aside 'TopK / JumpReLU drop the lambda term and enforce sparsity structurally'. PAYOFF CALLOUT (top-right or a small badge): 'a few named, active features = a monitoring & steering vocabulary; add alpha x (a feature's decoder direction) to steer behavior'. The whole point the layout must SELL is the contrast: a dense messy input on the left becoming a short list of a few clean, human-named features in the middle. Theme-safe: at most two accent hues (active feature vs inactive/zero), no hardcoded primary colors; keep all cell labels, the wide sparse column, and the addition inset inside the viewBox with no overlapping text; clear left-to-right reading order.

summary: The chapter already has three solid, theme-correct figures covering the residual-stream bus (S1.1), the activation-patching two-site recovery heatmap (S4), and the IOI circuit (S5); the heatmap and IOI figures are keeps and the residual-stream-bus figure needs a light refine (dark-mode annotation/bracket overlap, redundant cross-position arrows, and no concrete depiction of the virtual-weight write-then-read-a-subspace idea). The two genuinely foundational gaps are SUPERPOSITION (S1.3) - the most load-bearing concept in the chapter and currently unillustrated, best served by a static three-panel geometry diagram (orthogonal ideal -> non-orthogonal sparse packing -> polysemantic neuron) - and the SAE DECOMPOSITION (S6.1), a static pipeline showing a dense polysemantic activation re-expressed as a short sparse sum of monosemantic feature directions, which closes the loop opened by superposition and grounds the steering/monitoring payoff. Both new figures are static because a single well-composed frame conveys each intuition; no long-animation is warranted here.
